protocol.name: meek_shell_v1
protocol.copyright: SPDX-License-Identifier: MIT Copyright (c) 2026 the meek project contributors.

description.summary: private meek-shell <-> meek-compositor bridge

protocol.description:
A privileged extension bound only by meek-shell, the sole client meek-compositor forwards other clients' buffers + input to.
The compositor gates the global on the connecting client's process identity (PID + /proc/PID/exe path match against a hardcoded allowlist). Any other client attempting to bind the global will be silently ignored by the registry; if it somehow obtains the global interface descriptor and tries to bind directly, the compositor posts a protocol error and disconnects.
Lifetime: one meek_shell_v1 object per compositor. Destroying it is equivalent to meek-shell exiting; the compositor will respawn meek-shell and the new instance will re-bind.
Version 1 keeps the surface minimal: events + requests needed to get a single-output single-shell setup rendering app windows as textures. Multi-output, clipboard, drag-and-drop, popover positioning, explicit damage hints, etc. are v2+ territory.

interface.name: meek_shell_v1

description.summary: per-shell state + event pump

interface.description: Bound once by meek-shell. Carries the two-way bridge: compositor -> shell events describing other clients' lifecycle + buffers + raw input; shell -> compositor requests routing input back to specific clients + closing clients.

request.name: destroy

description.summary: stop the bridge

request.description: Called on clean meek-shell shutdown. Compositor stops forwarding events and frees any queued toplevel_buffer fds. Does NOT close the still-connected normal-client windows; they just don't get forwarded anymore.

request.name: announce_ready

description.summary: shell has installed event handlers

request.description:
Between bind and announce_ready, the compositor queues toplevel_added events (in case shell still has initialization to do). On announce_ready the queue is flushed + realtime events start flowing.
On meek-shell crash + respawn this is also the trigger for replaying toplevel_added for every still-alive app so the new shell rebuilds its scene.

description.summary: forward pointer motion to a toplevel
forward pointer button press/release to a toplevel
forward keyboard key to the focused toplevel

request.name: close_toplevel

description.summary: ask the target client to close itself

request.description: Sends xdg_toplevel.close to the target client. Non-destructive: the client may ignore and keep running (save-prompt dialogs etc.). For forced termination see kill_toplevel below.

request.name: kill_toplevel

description.summary: force-kill the target client's process

request.description:
Resolves handle -> wl_client -> PID via wl_client_get_credentials, then sends SIGKILL to that PID. Used by the shell's swipe-up-to-dismiss gesture in the task switcher: the user expects the app to be GONE, not to politely-close-then-maybe-stay-running. SIGKILL bypasses the client's signal handlers + save-prompts entirely.
After the process dies, libwayland fires its EPIPE/destroy path which makes the compositor emit toplevel_removed; shell uses that to clean up the scene node. So callers do NOT need to also call close_toplevel - kill alone produces both the death and the cleanup notification.
bad_handle/dead_handle errors apply same as close_toplevel.

event.name: toplevel_added

description.summary: a new app window appeared

event.description:
Fires when a normal client creates an xdg_toplevel. Shell uses this to allocate a scene node even before the first buffer arrives.
After meek-shell crash + respawn + announce_ready, the compositor replays one of these for every still-alive toplevel so the new shell can reconstruct its scene.

arg.name: app_id
arg.summary: client's xdg_toplevel.set_app_id value; may be empty

description.summary: client retitled its window

event.name: toplevel_removed

description.summary: app closed or crashed

event.description: Shell should remove the scene node for this handle. Any pending route_* calls targeting this handle are a protocol error.

event.name: toplevel_buffer

description.summary: new pixel content for a toplevel

event.description:
Fires on every wl_surface.commit that attached a buffer. The fd is a dup of the client's dmabuf fd. Shell must eglCreateImage + glEGLImageTargetTexture2DOES it to get a GL texture. Shell owns the fd from here and must close it when done (typical: close immediately after import, EGL holds the reference).
`buffer_id` is an opaque token the compositor assigns to each distinct wl_buffer it forwards. Re-used when the client re-attaches the same wl_buffer (triple-buffer rotation). Shell uses it as the key for an EGLImage cache: first time a buffer_id is seen, import; next time, look up the cached texture + EGLImage and skip the eglCreateImage. Compositor emits `buffer_forgotten(buffer_id)` when the underlying wl_buffer is destroyed, at which point the shell must eglDestroyImage + glDeleteTextures for that cache entry.
v1 only forwards single-plane dmabuf. Shm buffers upload CPU-side on compositor first + then send the resulting dmabuf (or, in the dev path, fall back to sending the shm pool fd; implementation-defined).
Multi-plane YUV is deferred to v2.

arg.name: buffer_id
arg.summary: compositor-assigned, stable across re-attaches of the same wl_buffer

event.name: buffer_forgotten

description.summary: a previously-seen buffer_id is gone

event.description: The wl_buffer that was assigned this buffer_id has been destroyed by the client. Shell must drop its cached EGLImage + GL texture for that id; the id may be re-used by the compositor for a future buffer.

event.name: toplevel_buffer_shm

description.summary: new pixel content for a toplevel, CPU-side buffer

event.description:
Same role as toplevel_buffer, for clients that render via wl_shm instead of linux-dmabuf-v1 (notably foot, weston- simple-shm, and any client without hardware buffer support).
Compositor has already mmapped the client's shm pool + read the pixels; it then writes them into a fresh memfd and passes THAT fd to the shell. Shell mmaps the forwarded fd read-only, uploads via glTexImage2D, and closes the fd immediately after upload (Mesa caches the texture until glDeleteTextures).
`buffer_id` has the same semantics as toplevel_buffer: the compositor assigns a stable id per underlying wl_buffer and the shell uses it as a texture-cache key. Matching buffer_forgotten fires on wl_buffer destruction.
`format` is a `wl_shm` format code (WL_SHM_FORMAT_ARGB8888 = 0, WL_SHM_FORMAT_XRGB8888 = 1, etc.). It is NOT a DRM fourcc; the two enums differ. Shell picks the GL upload format accordingly -- on every modern driver GL_BGRA + GL_UNSIGNED_ BYTE matches both ARGB8888 and XRGB8888 in little-endian memory order.
No stride offset field: the memfd holds exactly `stride * height` bytes of pixels, starting at offset 0.

arg.name: fd
arg.summary: memfd holding a copy of the shm pixels

event.name: ime_request_on

description.summary: an app wants the on-screen keyboard

event.description: A zwp_text_input_v3 resource owned by app `app_handle` just called `enable`. Shell should display its on-screen keyboard and route key taps via `ime_commit_string`.

event.name: ime_request_off

description.summary: the app no longer wants the keyboard

event.description: Fires when the app's text_input is disabled, destroyed, or loses focus. Shell should hide its on-screen keyboard.

request.name: ime_commit_string

description.summary: deliver typed text to the IME-focused app

request.description: The shell's on-screen keyboard produced `text` (one or more UTF-8 codepoints). Compositor forwards as `zwp_text_input_v3.commit_string(text)` + `done(serial)` to the currently enabled text_input.

request.name: route_keyboard_key

description.summary: deliver a key press/release to the focused app

request.description:
Shell's widget_keyboard produced a physical-level key event. Compositor forwards as wl_keyboard.key on every wl_keyboard resource owned by the target app's client. `keycode` is a Linux evdev keycode (KEY_A = 30, KEY_B = 48, etc.). `state`: 0 = released, 1 = pressed (matches wl_keyboard enum).
`app_handle` identifies the target. If 0, routes to the most-recently-focused app (whatever we stored as the keyboard focus target when the user tapped a tile to go fullscreen).
Use together with route_keyboard_modifiers when the shell needs to simulate shifted / ctrl'd keys.

request.name: route_keyboard_modifiers

description.summary: update the keyboard modifier state for the focused app

request.description: Same target selection as route_keyboard_key. The four bitfields match wl_keyboard.modifiers: depressed / latched / locked / group. Shell sends before any key event that uses a shift / ctrl / etc. held-modifier; resets to 0 when the modifier release completes.

event.name: frame_presented

description.summary: shell's last commit is now on screen

event.description: Fires after the DRM page-flip ack (or after eglSwapBuffers on the nested X11 dev backend). Shell uses this as its "draw the next frame" cue.

entry.name: not_allowed
entry.summary: client tried to bind but isn't the allowed shell

entry.name: bad_handle
entry.summary: route_* / close_toplevel referenced an unknown handle

entry.name: dead_handle
entry.summary: route_* targeted a toplevel that's been removed

entry.name: already_ready
entry.summary: announce_ready called twice on the same bridge
